texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since the beginning of industrial revolution in 19th century which was accompanied with a wide use of fossil fuel like coal and later on oil and gas, Earth's atmosphere has accumulated large amounts of greenhouse gases. The increase of carbon dioxide concentration from 310 to 400 ppm in the last 50 years is considered to be responsible for 1 1 C increase of the atmosphere temperature [1,2]. Reduction of carbon dioxide emission is considered as a major goal in order to avoid drastic climate change, which can occur if atmospheric temperature would rise for 2 1 C [3].

texts.orig: In 2010, electricity and heat production consumed 4840 million tons of oil equivalent (MTOE) of coal (46.5%), natural gas (22.8%) and oil (5.7%) as well as of nuclear (14.9%) and renewable energy (10.2%) [4]. Power generation is the most stable and stationary source of carbon dioxide emission and thus attracts attention as a candidate for carbon capture and storage (CCS).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane separation of fossil fuel fi red power plants off-gases has been extensively studied during the last decade and compared to other techniques such as cryogenic air separation, adsorption and absorption [5 -7]. It is acknowledged that the implementation of a carbon capture technology in energy production will require signi fi cant investments, operational expenses and thus increase of energy cost [5]. Gas separation will be the most expensive part of the CCS process.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Reduction of CO2 emission caused by fossil fuel power plants is widely appreciated task. The most suitable materials for off-gas membrane separation process are poly(ethylene glycol) containing polymers. Pebax s 1657 blended with low molecular weight poly(ethylene glycol)s is a promising material for the selective layer of thin fi lm composite gas separation membranes. Stability of blend Pebax s 1657 thick membranes was evaluated at temperatures up to 90 1 C, exceeding those at a stack of power plant. The membranes were tested for gas transport properties by thermoanalytical methods. It was found that a threshold of molecular weight exists between 350 and 500 g/mol, at which leaching out of the low molecular weight poly(ethylene glycol) compound from the polymer matrix becomes minimal. Poly(ethylene glycol) with methyl end groups having a molecular weight of 500 g/mol provides a blend material with a CO2 permeability coef fi cient higher than in case of Polyactive ™ . PEBAX s /DM500 blend can be a cheaper alternative to tailor made Polyactive ™ in large scale production of CO2 selective thin fi lm composite membrane.

texts.orig: Three main strategies are studied for CCS: pre-combustion, oxyfuel and post-combustion [8]. Polymeric membranes are considered as possible candidates for H2/CO2 separation at 200 -400 1 C of pre-combustion process [9 -14] and particularly for CO2/ N2 separation at moderate temperatures of post-combustion process [15,16]. Ceramic membranes able to withstand high temperatures of oxy-fuel (about 800 1 C) or pre-combustion processes are under development [17 -19].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Post-combustion separation to be carried out at power plant stack conditions will deal with enormous feed fl ow rate at pressure equal or slightly above atmospheric, with low carbon dioxide concentration not exceeding 15 vol%. Off-gas is at 100% of relative humidity and contains up to 60 mg/m 3 of solid impurities which will be accumulated on the frontal side of the membrane module if not removed beforehand. Small amounts of sulfur and nitrogen oxides present a signi fi cant challenge for the membrane stability, especially at conditions when water could condense on the feed side of the membrane [16]. Nevertheless successful pilot tests of post-combustion membrane separation with polymeric membranes are reported [20,21]. Development of the postcombustion membrane separation system will provide an opportunity of modernization of numerous existing power stations leading to signi fi cant CO2 emission reduction.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: strongly dependent on membrane permeance and according to calculations using parameters of existing membranes it will be about one million square meters for a 600 MW power plant. Low feed pressure requires a vacuum scheme of membrane use and thus process selectivity and energy demand will depend on the vacuum created on permeate side of the membrane and pressure losses inside the membrane module. The energy required for the separation is mostly determined by the selectivity of the membrane: in order to match the selectivity of the process to the high selectivity of the membrane a high vacuum will be necessary. But taking into account high feed and consequently high permeate fl ow rates creation of high vacuum on the permeate side is of technical problem. Existing steam ejector vacuum pumps able to deal with gas streams of 10 6 m 3 /h can provide about 100 mbar of absolute pressure [23] which leaves the process developer with the feed/permeate pressure ratio of approximately 10. As it was demonstrated in e.g. [24] at such pressure ratio increase of the possible membrane selectivity above α (CO2/N2) ¼ 60 does not bring signi fi cant improvement to the concentration of the CO2 in permeate. Economic evaluation of the postcombustion process shows that membranes with higher selectivity can reduce the operating costs (energy required for pumps operation) while increasing demand for the installed membrane area or investment costs. The cost of using the membrane separation system is expected to reduce the net ef fi ciency of the power plant from 45.9% to 38.9% in case of 95% purity and 78% recovery of CO2 [16,25,26].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Due to the limitations of the separation process the membrane module should give as small resistance to the feed fl ow as possible, should have as large packing density as viable, and should demand low production and installation costs. Most probably modules equipped with fl at sheet membranes will be advantageous compared to hollow fi ber modules because of the well-de fi ned fl uid dynamics in the module and effectiveness of membrane use. Additionally, it has to be mentioned that today there are no hollow fi ber membranes having a rubbery selective layer produced in pilot scale with permeances competing to fl at sheet membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane materials having two transport mechanisms are under consideration for post-combustion application: (i) active or facilitated transport, where speci fi c interactions between CO2 and membrane material provide fast CO2 molecule ' hopping ' from one active center to another, thus enforcing high selectivity; (ii) ' passive ' transport according to the solution -diffusion mechanism where polymers or organic compounds having af fi nity toward CO2 provide a relatively moderate selectivity. Other approaches such as membrane contactors [27] or supported liquid membranes [28] most probably will not reach application stage in the near future.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Moderate selectivity of polymeric membrane materials for CO2/ N2 separation is graphically presented as upper bound of Robeson plot [29] from which it is evident that CO2/N2 selectivity for polymers does not exceeds 90. Active transport materials demonstrate signi fi cantly higher selectivity, e.g. 160 for samples prepared as membranes for pilot scale experiments [30,31]. Unfortunately these membranes still have relatively thick separation layers and thus low permeance of about 1 m 3 (STP)/(m 2 h bar). Permeance can exceed 10 m 3 (STP)/(m 2 h bar) when advanced coating techniques are used during the membrane preparation are used [32]. If active transport membranes will be proven to be stable against the post-combustion feed stream, and the technique to keep the feed stream close to 100% of relative humidity throughout the module as required for the facilitated transport will be developed then such membranes will be very competitive candidates for industrial separation systems.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG) containing compounds are widely studied as materials for CO2 selective membranes [6,33]. Combination of relatively high permeability and selectivity bring polymers containing ethylene glycol moiety to or above the present upper bound for CO2/N2 in the Robeson plot. Fine tuning of polymer composition, adjustment of block length, or blending with low molecular weight poly(ethylene glycol)s (LMWPEGs) allow for signi fi cant increase of permeability without decrease in selectivity [34 -37].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: At least two thin fi lm composite (TFC) membranes based on PEG containing polymers have been examined in post-combustion CO2/ N2 pilot scale separation. Polaris ™ of MTR (Menlo Park, USA) and Polyactive ™ TFC membrane of Helmholtz-Zentrum Geesthacht have been tested at power plants and showed stable results [33,38 -40]. A 12 m 2 membrane module with Polyactive ™ TFC membrane is currently tested in the off-gas separation system on a coal fi red power plant within the frames of METPORE II project.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It was shown that PEG based membranes can match economic requirements of post-combustion separation. Realistic analysis of the separation process indicates that currently it is not possible to reach a CO2 purity suf fi cient for transportation and storage in a one stage separation [16]. In case of a two stage separation process, poly (ethylene glycol) based membranes can provide the necessary CO2 purity. Properties of already developed Polaris ™ and Polyactive ™ membranes are fi tting to the optimum region for industrial membrane application but can be further improved in terms of permeance which will result in reduced required membrane area [40].

texts.orig: High performance of the Polyactive ™ TFC membrane is based on low (50 -80 nm) thickness of the selective layer [41,42]. The thinnest TFC membrane prepared from such a block copolymer had an effective thickness of the selective layer in the range of 30 nm [43]. Further improvement of the membrane and separation process can be done by reduction of membrane cost. Polyactive ™ is relatively expensive polymer and its substitution with cheaper materials such as Pebax s MH1657 would be economically bene fi cial.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As it was reported before, blend materials of Pebax s 1657 and poly(ethylene glycol) of different molecular weights and nature of end groups show very promising results for use as membrane selective layers [44]. Poly(ethylene glycol) functionalized POSS [45] and PDMS [37] give very promising gas transport property change to Pebax s when blended with it. Blends of low molecular weight compounds and polymers are known to be unstable in time and leaching out of low molecular weight compounds is well known in e.g. rubber technology [46 -49]. Herein we have studied blends having a high content of different molecular weights and nature of end groups LMWPEGs. Rapid leaching of low molecular weight PEG compounds was induced by heating to 90 1 C, the temperature exceeding possible application range, while measuring the gas transport properties of a  100 m m thick fi lm of the material. Changes in the blends composition were studied by weighing, thermographic and calorimetric techniques. A threshold of the LMWPEG molecular weight was observed at which leaching out of LMWPEG was stopped and stable polymer blend membranes based on Pebax could be formed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Gas transport properties were determined for fi lms having a thickness of approximately 100 m m which were prepared by casting from polymer solution. Pebax s was dissolved in a mixture of ethanol/water (70/30 wt%) by stirring at 80 1 C for 3 h in order to obtain 3 wt% solution [44]. For preparation of Pebax s blended with LMWPEG fi lms the polymer solution was cooled down and then the required amount of LMWPEG was added to achieve 40 wt % of the total solids content. The solution was vigorously stirred for at least 30 min, fi ltered and poured into the aluminum ring placed on a leveled Te fl on s coated glass plate kept at 30 1 C. Ethanol/ water solvent was evaporated during 36 -48 h. For dust protection the aluminum ring was covered with polyester non-woven.

Polyactive ™ fi lms were prepared by the same procedure except temperature was kept at 25 1 C and the aluminum ring was covered with a glass cup providing a small gap between the cup and the ring in order to allow for a slow evaporation of THF (at least 36 h).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Detailed studies of the properties of Pebax s and Polyactive ™ were performed by Car et al. [54]. As reported, Pebax s and Polyactive ™ have high CO2 selectivities over N2 and H2. Their CO2 permeability can be improved by blending with LMWPEG without affecting the selectivity. Both of these commercially available polymers contain fl exible, rubbery PEG segments, which amount to 60 wt% for Pebax s and 77 wt% for Polyactive ™ . The glass transition temperature Tg of PEG blocks having similar size of about 1500 g/mol was measured at � 53 1 C and at � 49 1 C, respectively, which can be related to narrower PEG block molecular weight distribution in Polyactive ™ resulting in different PEG packings [37,55].

Their CO2 permeability can be improved by blending with LMWPEG without affecting the selectivity. Both of these commercially available polymers contain fl exible, rubbery PEG segments, which amount to 60 wt% for Pebax s and 77 wt% for Polyactive ™ . The glass transition temperature Tg of PEG blocks having similar size of about 1500 g/mol was measured at � 53 1 C and at � 49 1 C, respectively, which can be related to narrower PEG block molecular weight distribution in Polyactive ™ resulting in different PEG packings [37,55].

texts.orig: Samples of thick fi lms were prepared with a thickness of about 100 m maccording to the method described above and tested of the gas transport properties in the temperature range 30 -90 1 C. This temperature range was chosen in order to study stability of the material at temperatures exceeding those of possible operation conditions at power plant stack where the temperature can be as high as 65 1 C. High temperature was expected to provoke composition changes within the blend materials of the study, undesired but possible to occur during long operation under industrial conditions. The preparation of the blended membranes was done according to the previous works of Yave et al. [35] and Car et al. [44] where gas transport experiments were carried out within the temperature range 10 -60 1 C.

texts.orig: ATR-FRIR analyses were conducted to monitor the presence of residual solvents such as water in the membranes. It was found that the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of water (v3, H -O -H ), a peak which is very broad, at approximately 3700 cm � 1 , was very weak although it is well known that a broad and strong peak is expected when even a small amount of water is incorporated into the sample. Additionally no further peak at 1500 cm � 1 due to the water bending vibration (v2) was observed, con fi rming complete solvent evaporation during membrane preparation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Initial gas transport tests were carried out with stepwise temperature decrease from 80 to 30 1 C. Such experiments would exclude in fl uence of the membrane preparation history on gas transport parameters. It was found that the results obtained differ signi fi cantly from those reported earlier [44]. At the end of experiment at 30 1 C permeability coef fi cients lower than expected were found for blends containing DM250 LMWPEG reaching values of Pebax s . It was concluded that membrane can lose LMW component at elevated temperature due to high mobility of LMWPEG molecules in Pebax s matrix. In order to con fi rm the hypothesis the gas transport experiments with stepwise heating from 30 to 90 1 C and consecutive cooling back to 30 1 C were carried out for membranes containing the same amount (40 wt%) of LMWPEGs of different molecular weights and end group nature.

texts.orig: Several fi lms of Pebax s having thicknesses varying from 80 to 140 m mwere formed to prove reproducibility of the fi lm preparation technique and independence of measured gas transport parameters on membrane thickness. Although the fi lm thickness can vary up to 7 5 m m, the large number of thickness measurement points have assured obtaining representative average membrane thickness which resulted in values of permeability and diffusion coef fi cients matching to those for fi lms of other thicknesses and to values reported in the literature [55]. Pebax s membranes demonstrated good stability during gas transport experiments: the deviation in permeability coef fi cients for CO2 was less than 2% for points obtained at the same temperature during the heating and cooling parts of experiment. The standard deviation of permeability coef fi cient for each temperature point for e.g. a fi lm having thickness of 107 m mwas not more than 0.07% for 3 consecutive measurements.

texts.orig: coef fi cients of all gases except hydrogen for Polyactive ™ are 2 -2.4 times higher than those for Pebax s due to higher content of more regular PEG in the Polyactive ™ and, probably due to the difference in chemical structure of the hard blocks (poly(butylene terephthalate) in Polyactive ™ and Nylon 6 in Pebax s ). Higher PEG blocks content provokes higher mobility of all gases in the polymer matrix as can be seen from comparison of diffusion coef fi cients. Only methane shows lower molecule mobility in Polyactive ™ compared to Pebax s which is compensated by signi fi cantly higher solubility coef fi cient calculated according to Eq. (3). Higher CH4 solubility and its partial immobilization (lower diffusion coef fi cient) in Polyactive ™ can be related to the presence of butyl moiety in the hard block of the polymer providing higher polymer af fi nity toward CH4 and probably answering question of hard block participation in gas transport process [35].

texts.orig: For all measured samples discrepancies between results obtained during the heating and cooling parts of experiments were observed. The greatest deviation has been found in the case of LMWPEG additives with the lowest molecular weights, namely PEG DM250 and PEG DV240. The lowest deviation was observed for fi lms with LMWPEG additives having molecular weights of 500 g/mol or higher. Temperature dependencies of CO2 permeability coef fi cient typical for all studied materials are presented in Fig. 3. For comparison purposes and since the aim of the work is the preparation of a material having properties superior to Polyactive ™ , the curves for pristine Pebax s and Polyactive ™ are given. It can be seen that for all studied materials the permeability coef fi cient obtained during the heating part at 30 1 C is higher than for pure polymers. But during the temperature increase the curve does not follow an exponential dependence as expected for thermally activated processes. In case of DM250 the CO2 permeability coef fi cient is gradually decreasing with temperature and for DV240 the curve has a turning point at 50 1 C. In case of DM500 the heating and cooling parts are close to each other. Such behavior as presented in Fig. 3 has led us to the conclusion that blend samples undergo severe changes in composition during the temperature treatment associated with gas transport experiments. texts.orig: TGA experiments carried out on pure Pebax s and LMWPEGs with molecular weights of 250 and 500 g/mol, as well as on the thick fi lms of their blends con fi rmed the data obtained from membrane weighing. During the heating from room temperature to 600 1 C LMWPEG was losing the mass in one step and Pebax s in two steps which is attributable to the presence of two sorts of blocks in the multiblock copolymer. No evidence of water or other volatile solvent was observed and there was no remaining mass at the end of the measurement. It is worth to be mentioned that the degradation temperature of the low molecular weight substances is much lower compared to the one of the commercial polymers. TGA experiments (Fig. 5) carried out for the membranes with the DM250 showed signi fi cant differences in the content of LMWPEG before and after gas transport experiments quantitatively con fi rming the observation of fi lm weight loss. The summary of the weight loss [wt%] for fi lms of Pebax s originally containing 40 wt% LMWPEG of different molecular weights is shown in Table 2. Samples containing the DM1000 are not listed in the table since all prepared fi lms were too brittle to withstand mechanical stress caused by used experimental setup. It was also found that thicker Pebax s based membranes (  200 m m) are losing less LMWPEG due to diffusion restrictions. Films of Pebax s with DM500 having thickness of  224 m m and  50 m m lose 5 wt% and 14 wt% respectively during the time-lag experiment. Since the aim of the work was to fi nd limits of blend stability, it was decided to focus on membranes where leaching out could occur during the time frames of a standard program for a gas transport experiment. At the same time, the thickness of the sample should be big enough to obtain statistically relevant data on weight loss for blends containing various LMWPEGs. From numerous experiments it was found that the thickness of about 100 m m(this corresponds to approximately 10 -20 thousand times the radius of gyration of a PEG chain) ensures good and reproducible results of all tests.

texts.orig: Additional DSC experiments with different temperature programs were carried out for three blend membranes of Pebax s with DM250, AM350 and DM500, in order to examine the difference of the Tc for the LMWPEG. The temperature program started with a heating from room temperature up to 120 1 C and then cooling down to � 100 1 C. Afterwards, a heating ( � 100 1 C to 120 1 C), a new cooling and fi nally a last heating/cooling cycle completed the thermal analysis. The important fi nding in case of Pebax s blended with DM250, was the shifting of the PEG crystallization temperature from the fi rst cooling ( � 17.5 1 C) to the second cooling ( � 4.6 1 C). Similar results were found in case of AM350, where the shift was from � 7 1 C during the fi rst cooling and � 2 1 C at the second cooling which is signi fi cant but smaller than in case of DM250. Important is to be mentioned that the second and third heating/cooling cycles were identical and all the other thermal transitions were observed at the same temperatures as given in Table 4. For comparison reasons the Pebax s blend membrane with DM500 was also tested with the same temperature program but no difference in Tc was found for all cooling parts of thermal cycles. From the additional DSC analysis it is observed that the threshold for the leaching out of the LMWPEGs as a function of the molecular weight is between 350 g/mol and 500 g/ mol, as was already discussed in the previous section of this work.

texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Gas transport parameters of Pebax s , Polyactive ™ and blend materials of Pebax s with LMWPEGs of different molecular weights have been studied in a wide temperature range exceeding possible application temperature. It was found that blends with LMWPEGs having molecular weight less than 500 g/mol are unstable at elevated temperatures, tend to lose most of low molecular weight component and thus cannot be chosen as materials for the selective layer of TFC membranes. LMWPEGs form stable blends with Pebax s in which equilibrium fraction of low molecular weight component is between 30 and 33 wt%. The blend of Pebax s and PEG DM500 has properties superior to Polyactive ™ .
texts.text: Gas transport parameters of Pebax s , Polyactive ™ and blend materials of Pebax s with LMWPEGs of different molecular weights have been studied in a wide temperature range exceeding possible application temperature. It was found that blends with LMWPEGs having molecular weight less than 500 g/mol are unstable at elevated temperatures, tend to lose most of low molecular weight component and thus cannot be chosen as materials for the selective layer of TFC membranes. LMWPEGs form stable blends with Pebax s in which equilibrium fraction of low molecular weight component is between 30 and 33 wt%. The blend of Pebax s and PEG DM500 has properties superior to Polyactive ™ .

texts.orig: TGA experiments have con fi rmed the leaching out of the LMWPEGs from the Pebax s matrix. DSC studies have shown that LMWPEGs are dissolved in the PEG domains of the multiblock copolymer.

texts.orig: The data obtained during gas transport experiments at temperatures from 30 to 90 1 C will allow quality control of TFC membranes to be developed. Results obtained on blend stability are to be studied further when blend materials will be implemented as a selective layer of multilayer TFC membranes.
